title: Preach the Word
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro

outline.title: Introduction: Why Do We Have Preaching?
subpoints: Preaching may seem like an outdated mode of communication — why not drama, multimedia, or other formats?
The answer is that God speaks through preaching: in creation, in redemption, God spoke; he created by speaking and he redeemed his people by the power of his word
Throughout the Old Testament: 'thus says the Lord' — the spoken word was appointed as the center of the life of the church
God illustrated this in Ezekiel 37: he took his prophet to a valley of dry bones and commanded him to preach — life came through the proclaimed word
God appointed preaching knowing every technology available; we cannot outdo God's appointed means

outline.title: 1. Scripture Is God Speaking
subpoints: 2 Timothy 3:16: 'all Scripture is given by inspiration of God' — theopneustos, God-breathed; the breathing out of God is his speaking
God spoke all the words of the Bible — not only the concepts but the very words; from Genesis 1 to Revelation 22, this is God speaking
Evidence: in the New Testament, theological arguments are built on a single letter of the Old Testament (e.g., Paul arguing from 'seed' singular vs. 'seeds' plural — Galatians 3:16)
When Scripture is preached, God himself speaks to his congregation; the Reformers understood this — grace is dispensed especially in the preaching of the Word
Scripture equips the man of God to be complete, thoroughly equipped for every good work — it is through the Word that we find what God calls us to do and are empowered to do it

outline.title: 2. The Command to Preach
subpoints: Paul puts Timothy under a solemn charge — before God and the Lord Jesus Christ, who will judge the living and the dead — to preach the Word
Timothy appears to have been timid and fearful of people; Paul's answer: your judge is God, not the congregation
'Be ready in season and out of season' — the only two times a pastor must preach; 'season' has nothing to do with the year but with whether it is convenient or inconvenient
The preacher must convince, rebuke, and exhort with all longsuffering and teaching — not merely comfort

A pastor is more than a preacher — he must shepherd the flock — but he is not less than a preacher; one of the greatest ways a pastor can love his congregation is to prepare to proclaim the Word of God

outline.title: 3. The Congregation's Responsibility
subpoints: 2 Timothy 4:3-4 addresses the people in the pew, not the unbelieving world: 'they will not endure sound doctrine... they will heap up for themselves teachers'
It is on the congregation's shoulders to make sure the preaching from the pulpit is faithful to the Word of God
When preaching degenerates into ear-scratching, it happens because the people have allowed and even demanded it
History repeatedly shows churches and denominations turning to 'fables' — liberalism, Unitarianism — when the people turned their ears from the truth
The congregation of the Lord Jesus Christ bears real responsibility to preserve faithful preaching

summary: An afternoon sermon on 2 Timothy 3:16–4:5, explaining why preaching is central to the life of the church. God speaks through preaching — he created and redeemed by speaking, illustrated sovereign life-giving through Ezekiel's valley of dry bones, and appointed the proclaimed word as the center of church life across all generations and technologies. Scripture is God-breathed (theopneustos) — the very words are God's speech, down to single letters upon which New Testament arguments are built. Paul charges Timothy under a solemn oath before the divine Judge to preach the Word in season and out of season. The sermon closes with an unexpected application: the congregation, not only the pastor, bears responsibility for the faithfulness of the preaching they receive. Churches that drift into error do so because the people demand ear-scratching over sound doctrine.
transcript:
## Introduction: Why Do We Have Preaching?

We have grown used to preaching in our church — it seems to be how things have always been, and sometimes we may not even question ourselves: why do we have preaching? Why have a man up front speaking while everyone sits and listens? Why not something more dynamic? Drama, perhaps, or multimedia?

The reason we have preaching as part of the worship service is because the Word of God tells us to do so. But more than that, it is because God speaks — that is why we have preaching. In creation, in redemption, God speaks. He spoke creation into existence, and Paul in 2 Corinthians picks up on that: he redeemed his people by the power of his word. Throughout the Old Testament we have God saying, 'Thus says the Lord' — the spoken word appointed as the means by which God ministers to his people.

And God wanted to give an illustration of how he brings people to life. He takes Ezekiel to a valley of dry bones and tells him to preach to the dry bones. Life came through the proclaimed word. Preaching to dying people is exactly what it is, because God instituted it.

God knew what technologies were going to develop. He knows all that will be available a thousand years from now if the Lord has not returned. And yet he appointed the preaching of his word. We cannot outdo God in his appointed means.

## 1. Scripture Is God Speaking

Why did God appoint preaching? Because the Bible itself is God speaking.

Verse 16 says: *All Scripture is given by inspiration of God.* The word translated 'inspiration' or 'breathed out' is the Greek compound *theopneustos* — God breathed. The breathing out is speaking. God spoke all the words we have in our Bible. From Genesis 1 to Revelation 22, this is God speaking. Not just the concepts — the very words used. It is often the case that theological arguments in the New Testament are made from a single letter of the Old Testament. Paul argues in Galatians that God said 'seed' singular, not 'seeds' plural — and builds a whole theological argument on the difference. The very words are God's.

So when we preach the Bible, the act of preaching itself is God speaking to us. The Reformers understood this. Our confession says that it is in the reading of the Word, especially in the preaching of the Word, that grace is dispensed — grace made available to God's people.

The Word of God is also profitable: for doctrine, for reproof, for correction, for instruction in righteousness — that the man of God may be complete, thoroughly equipped for every good work. It is through the Word that we find what God calls us to do, and through the Word that we are empowered to do it.

## 2. The Command to Preach

In chapter 4 verse 1, Paul puts Timothy under a solemn charge: *I charge you therefore, before God and the Lord Jesus Christ, who will judge the living and the dead at his appearing and his kingdom: preach the Word.*

Timothy appears to have been a timid man — perhaps given to fear of people. Paul's answer: are you afraid of what people might think? Are you afraid to say things that might upset them? Your judge is God. He is the one who will evaluate your preaching at the end of time. So preach the Word.

And not just preach — preach *the word*. Be ready in season and out of season. There are only two times a pastor must preach: when he is in season and when he is out of season. This has nothing to do with the calendar. The idea is: when people want what you have to give them, and when they don't. In season, when it is convenient. Out of season, when it is inconvenient. In both cases: preach.

In that preaching, he must convince, rebuke, and exhort — with all longsuffering and teaching. Not just encouragement. The full range.

A pastor is more than a preacher — a man who only stands and preaches is a preacher, not a pastor. But a pastor is not *less* than a preacher. One of the greatest ways a pastor can love his congregation is to prepare faithfully to proclaim the Word of God to them.

## 3. The Congregation's Responsibility

Now Paul turns back to Timothy — but what he says has everything to do with the people in the pew.

*For the time will come when they will not endure sound doctrine, but according to their own desires, because they have itching ears, they will heap up for themselves teachers; and they will turn their ears away from the truth, and be turned aside to fables.*

This is not describing the unbelieving world. This is describing professing Christians in the church — people in the pew. And notice: it is *on their shoulders*. They will heap up for themselves teachers. They will turn their ears away.

It is on the congregation's shoulders to make sure that the preaching from the pulpit is from the Word of God. When preaching becomes nothing more than ear-scratching, it is only because the congregation wanted it. You have allowed it to happen.

We see this in history again and again. The great Congregationalist churches became Unitarian. Mainline denominations turned liberal in the late 1800s. People turned to fables — and the fable is believing that Jesus is not God, that God does not save, that Scripture is not true. The mythical thing is what the Bible teaches. The fable is what we substitute for it.

Be watchful. Do the work of an evangelist. Fulfill your ministry.

My prayer for this church — as it has been for the last six years — is that this would be a congregation that desires the milk of the Word of God, that is zealous for the proclamation of the Word, that desires to see Christ more clearly every time we gather.

Father, thank you for your Word. Bless the preaching of your Word in this church and in every church that names the name of Jesus. Revive churches that have left the Scriptures. And make us a people who in every season — in season and out — hear and love and obey your proclaimed Word. In Jesus' name, amen.
